tribes.description: The Kanuri are heirs to the thousand-year Kanem-Bornu Empire, known for their Islamic scholarship, imperial heritage, and resilience facing the Lake Chad environmental crisis.

tribes.name: Moors (Beydane)

traditional.male: Men were warriors, scholars, and herders. Warrior groups (hassane) dominated politically. Scholarly groups (zawiya) held religious authority.
traditional.female: Women owned tents and household property. They composed poetry, played music, and had significant influence. Corpulence was traditionally prized.

modern.male: Modern Moor men work in government, business, and professions. Many maintain scholarly traditions.
modern.female: Women increasingly access education and politics. Traditional arts like music remain important.

funFacts: Mauritania was the last country to officially abolish slavery (1981, criminalized 2007)
Moor women traditionally gained status through corpulence - force-feeding (gavage) was practiced
Mauritanian tea ceremony involves three glasses - bitter, sweet, and perfumed
The Moors speak Hassaniya, a distinctive Arabic dialect with Berber influences
Mauritania has vast manuscript libraries with centuries of Islamic scholarship

traditionalFood.description: Moor cuisine combines nomadic traditions with influences from Senegal. Rice and meat dishes dominate. Camel milk and dates are staples. Three glasses of sweet tea is essential.

eatingCustoms.mealPattern: Light breakfast, lunch, evening meal
eatingCustoms.eatingStyle: Communal eating from large shared dish
eatingCustoms.genderRules: Men and women eat separately; guests are prioritized
eatingCustoms.specialOccasions: Elaborate feasts with mechoui for weddings and Islamic holidays
eatingCustoms.hospitality: Three glasses of tea ritual - essential hospitality

tribes.traditionalDance: Hawl (traditional music and dance gatherings)

traditionalReligion.name: Islam (Sunni Maliki)
traditionalReligion.beliefs: Moors are devout Muslims following the Maliki school. Sufi orders are influential. Marabouts (religious leaders) have significant social power.

religiousHistory.introducedBy: Arab and Berber conquerors/traders
religiousHistory.conversionProcess: Islam spread with the Arab-Berber ancestors of the Moors. Mauritania became a center of Islamic learning.

history.origin: The Moors descend from Arab-Berber groups who migrated to the western Sahara. They established the Almoravid Empire that conquered Morocco and Spain.
history.colonialEra: France colonized Mauritania in the late 19th-early 20th century, disrupting nomadic life and traditional power structures.
history.independence: Independent Mauritania (1960) has been dominated by Moor politics. The legacy of slavery and relations with Black Mauritanians remain sensitive issues.

linguisticLineage.migrationHistory: Hassaniya developed from Yemeni Arabic with Berber influences

tribes.description: The Moors are the Arab-Berber majority of Mauritania, known for their desert nomadism, Islamic scholarship, rich poetry, and complex social traditions spanning a millennium.

tribes.name: Haratin

traditional.male: Men worked in agriculture, herding, and labor. The legacy of slavery meant limited social mobility. Haratin men are now prominent in activism.
traditional.female: Women traditionally worked as domestics and farmers. They faced multiple oppressions but are increasingly vocal in anti-slavery movements.

modern.male: Modern Haratin men work in all sectors. Activists like Biram Dah Abeid have become internationally recognized for human rights work.
modern.female: Women increasingly access education and activism. They are central to movements challenging traditional hierarchies.

funFacts: Haratin means 'freed slaves' in Arabic - many are descendants of enslaved people
Mauritania didn't criminalize slavery until 2007 and practices continue
Biram Dah Abeid, a Haratin, is a globally recognized anti-slavery activist
Haratin speak Hassaniya but maintain distinct cultural identity
The Haratin movement is one of Africa's most important civil rights struggles

specialDishes: Similar to Moor cuisine but historically simpler fare
traditionalFood.description: Haratin cuisine is similar to Moor food but historically reflected their lower status. Agricultural products they grew were often consumed by masters.

eatingCustoms.mealPattern: Morning and evening meals
eatingCustoms.eatingStyle: Communal eating
eatingCustoms.genderRules: Men and women may eat separately
eatingCustoms.specialOccasions: Celebrations increasingly possible with improved status
eatingCustoms.hospitality: Hospitality important despite limited means

tribes.traditionalDance: Participation in traditional Mauritanian music

traditionalReligion.name: Islam (Sunni)
traditionalReligion.beliefs: Haratin are Muslims but historically were denied full religious education. Modern movements emphasize Islamic equality.

religiousHistory.introducedBy: Moor masters
religiousHistory.conversionProcess: Enslaved people were converted to Islam. Modern activists cite Islamic equality to challenge discrimination.

history.origin: Haratin are descendants of enslaved Black Africans who were assimilated into Moor culture over centuries. They speak Hassaniya but face ongoing discrimination.
history.colonialEra: French colonization nominally ended slavery but did little to change social structures. Haratin remained at the bottom of Mauritanian society.
history.independence: Post-independence, slavery continued despite abolitions in 1981 and criminalization in 2007. Haratin activism has grown significantly since the 2000s.

linguisticLineage.migrationHistory: Adopted Hassaniya through cultural assimilation

tribes.description: The Haratin are Mauritania's largest demographic group, descendants of enslaved people now at the forefront of one of the world's most important ongoing anti-slavery movements.

tribes.name: Soninke

traditional.male: Men dominated trade and migration. Soninke men traveled across Africa and now to Europe. They are expected to send remittances home.
traditional.female: Women maintain villages while men migrate. They farm, manage households, and maintain community bonds during long male absences.

modern.male: Soninke men are famous as migrant workers in France and other countries. Remittances support home communities.
modern.female: Women increasingly educated. They manage significant resources from remittances and are village decision-makers.

funFacts: The Soninke founded the Ghana Empire (not modern Ghana) - one of Africa's first great states
Soninke controlled the gold trade that made the Ghana Empire wealthy
There are more Soninke in France than in some African countries
Soninke villages often depend heavily on remittances from Europe
The famous Koumbi Saleh was the capital of Soninke Ghana

traditionalFood.description: Soninke cuisine is based on millet and sorghum. Fish from the Senegal River features prominently. Dembikere couscous is a specialty.

eatingCustoms.mealPattern: Morning porridge, lunch, evening meal
eatingCustoms.eatingStyle: Communal eating from shared dishes
eatingCustoms.genderRules: Men and women may eat separately; elders honored
eatingCustoms.specialOccasions: Elaborate feasts for weddings, Eid, returns of migrants
eatingCustoms.hospitality: Guests are treated generously - reflects on family honor

tribes.traditionalDance: Traditional dances at ceremonies and festivals

traditionalReligion.name: Pre-Islamic/Islam
traditionalReligion.beliefs: Soninke are predominantly Muslim but some pre-Islamic practices survive. The Ghana Empire initially practiced traditional religion before Islamic conversion.

religiousHistory.introducedBy: Trans-Saharan traders
religiousHistory.conversionProcess: The Ghana Empire fell partly due to Islamic pressure. Soninke gradually converted through trade and scholarship.

history.origin: The Soninke founded the Ghana Empire (c. 300-1200 CE), controlling the gold and salt trade. They were West Africa's first major state-builders.
history.colonialEra: French colonization fragmented Soninke across several colonies. Men began migrating to cities and eventually to France for work.
history.independence: Post-independence, Soninke remained divided across Mali, Mauritania, and Senegal. Migration to France intensified from the 1960s onward.

linguisticLineage.migrationHistory: Soninke spread through trade and empire

tribes.description: The Soninke are the founders of the ancient Ghana Empire, renowned for controlling West Africa's gold trade and now famous for their migration networks connecting Sahel villages to European cities.

tribes.name: Bambara (Bamana)

traditional.male: Men farmed, hunted, and went to war. Bambara kingdoms were male-led. The ton youth associations trained boys in community responsibility.
traditional.female: Women farmed, traded, and managed households. Certain crafts (pottery) were female. Women had significant economic autonomy through trade.

modern.male: Modern Bambara men dominate Malian politics, business, and professions. Farming remains important.
modern.female: Women increasingly educated. Many are traders and professionals while maintaining traditional roles.

funFacts: Bambara is the most widely spoken language in Mali, used as a lingua franca
The Bambara Empire of Ségou was one of the last to fall to French colonization
Bambara masks are world-famous and found in major museums
The N'Ko alphabet was created to write Bambara and related languages
Bambara hunter societies (donzo) are powerful traditional institutions

traditionalFood.description: Bambara cuisine centers on millet and sorghum. Tô (thick porridge) with various sauces is essential. Groundnuts and shea butter enrich dishes.

eatingCustoms.mealPattern: Morning porridge, midday meal, evening meal
eatingCustoms.eatingStyle: Communal eating from shared bowls
eatingCustoms.genderRules: Men and women often eat separately; order by age/status
eatingCustoms.specialOccasions: Elaborate feasts for marriages, funerals, and festivals
eatingCustoms.hospitality: Guests are honored - 'Teriya' (friendship) is essential

tribes.traditionalDance: Ton youth association dances, mask dances

traditionalReligion.name: Bambara Traditional Religion
traditionalReligion.beliefs: Bambara religion involves complex initiation societies and belief in nyama (spiritual force). Traditional practices remain strong even among Muslims.

religiousHistory.introducedBy: Muslim traders and scholars
religiousHistory.conversionProcess: Bambara resisted Islam longer than neighbors. The Ségou and Kaarta kingdoms were non-Muslim until the 19th century.

history.origin: The Bambara migrated from the Mande heartland and established the powerful kingdoms of Ségou and Kaarta in the 17th-19th centuries.
history.colonialEra: The Bambara kingdoms fell to French conquest in the late 19th century. Colonial rule disrupted traditional political structures but Bambara culture persisted.
history.independence: Post-independence Mali has been dominated by Bambara language and culture. Bambara became the de facto national language and Bamako is in Bambara territory.

linguisticLineage.migrationHistory: Bambara spread from the Mande heartland with empire expansion

tribes.description: The Bambara are Mali's largest ethnic group, founders of great kingdoms, and custodians of rich artistic and religious traditions that have shaped West African culture.

tribes.name: Dogon

traditional.male: Men farmed, carved, and performed mask dances. The hogon (spiritual leader) and elders made decisions. Initiation societies structured male life.
traditional.female: Women farmed, made pottery, brewed beer, and managed households. Menstruation huts were part of traditional life.

modern.male: Modern Dogon men farm, work in tourism, and maintain craft traditions. Many guide tourists to cliff villages.
modern.female: Women continue traditional roles. Tourism provides additional income opportunities.

funFacts: Dogon claim ancient knowledge of Sirius B, the invisible companion star of Sirius
The Bandiagara Escarpment villages are a UNESCO World Heritage Site
Dogon masks represent over 80 different characters
Dogon cosmology describes the universe with remarkable complexity
The Dogon fled to the cliffs to resist Islamization centuries ago

traditionalFood.description: Dogon cuisine is based on millet grown on cliff terraces. Onions are a major crop and export. Dolo beer is important in ceremonies.

eatingCustoms.mealPattern: Morning meal, evening meal
eatingCustoms.eatingStyle: Communal eating, specific utensils for ritual foods
eatingCustoms.genderRules: Men and women often eat separately; ritual rules during ceremonies
eatingCustoms.specialOccasions: Elaborate feasts for dama funerals and Sigui ceremonies
eatingCustoms.hospitality: Visitors welcomed with dolo and food

tribes.traditionalDance: Mask dances at dama funerals and festivals

traditionalReligion.name: Dogon Religion
traditionalReligion.beliefs: Dogon religion has a complex cosmology involving Amma, the Nommo water spirits, and celestial knowledge. The Sigui ceremony occurs every 60 years to renew the world.

religiousHistory.introducedBy: Muslim traders and neighbors
religiousHistory.conversionProcess: Dogon resisted Islam for centuries by retreating to cliffs. Conversion has accelerated recently but traditional practices remain strong.

history.origin: The Dogon migrated to the Bandiagara cliffs between the 13th-15th centuries, possibly fleeing Islamization. They displaced or absorbed the Tellem people.
history.colonialEra: French colonization had limited impact on remote Dogon villages. Traditional structures largely survived.
history.independence: Post-independence, Dogon villages became major tourist attractions. Recent jihadist violence has severely affected the region.

linguisticLineage.migrationHistory: Dogon languages form their own family, possibly related to Niger-Congo

famousPeople.name: Ogotemmêli
famousPeople.role: Sage who revealed Dogon cosmology to Marcel Griaule

tribes.description: The Dogon are famous for their cliff-dwelling villages on the Bandiagara Escarpment, complex cosmology, and spectacular mask traditions that have captivated scholars and visitors for generations.

tribes.name: Songhai

traditional.male: Men traded, fished, and served as scholars in the great empire. Askia Muhammad established Islamic learning centers.
traditional.female: Women processed fish, made pottery, traded, and managed households. They had significant economic roles in river trade.

modern.male: Modern Songhai men work in trade, professions, and government. Fishing and farming continue.
modern.female: Women access education and formal employment while maintaining traditional economic activities.

funFacts: The Songhai Empire was larger than Western Europe at its peak
Timbuktu was one of the world's great centers of learning with thousands of manuscripts
Askia Muhammad made a famous pilgrimage to Mecca with tons of gold
The Songhai developed sophisticated bureaucracy and taxation systems
Morocco conquered the empire in 1591 with firearms

traditionalFood.description: Songhai cuisine centers on Niger River fish and rice. Dried and smoked fish are specialties. Rice cultivation along the river is ancient.

eatingCustoms.mealPattern: Morning meal, lunch, evening meal
eatingCustoms.eatingStyle: Communal eating from shared dishes
eatingCustoms.genderRules: Men and women often eat separately; age hierarchy observed
eatingCustoms.specialOccasions: Elaborate feasts for Islamic holidays and life events
eatingCustoms.hospitality: Guests are generously treated

tribes.traditionalDance: Traditional dances at festivals and ceremonies

traditionalReligion.name: Islam (Sunni)
spirits: Some pre-Islamic spirits survive in folk belief
traditionalReligion.beliefs: Songhai are devout Muslims with a proud tradition of Islamic scholarship centered on Timbuktu.

religiousHistory.introducedBy: Trans-Saharan traders
religiousHistory.conversionProcess: Islam became the state religion under Askia Muhammad (1493-1528). Timbuktu became a center of Islamic learning.

history.origin: The Songhai built one of Africa's greatest empires (c. 1464-1591), controlling the trans-Saharan trade and making Timbuktu a world center of learning.
history.colonialEra: French colonization fragmented Songhai across Mali and Niger. The empire's decline had already occurred centuries earlier after Moroccan invasion.
history.independence: Post-independence, Songhai remain divided between Mali and Niger. They maintain cultural and linguistic unity despite borders.

linguisticLineage.migrationHistory: Songhai spread along the Niger River with empire expansion

tribes.description: The Songhai are heirs to one of Africa's greatest empires, which at its height controlled the trans-Saharan trade and made Timbuktu a world center of Islamic learning.
